// Generated by GitHub Copilot plan mode with requirements.prd.md as an input

# multirepo-mcp Implementation Plan

## Problem statement

Build a streamable-HTTP MCP server (`multirepo-mcp`) in C# / .NET 10 that lets AI agents read content from GitHub repositories across multiple organizations. Authentication to GitHub is performed as a **GitHub App** (not a PAT), with installation access tokens (IATs) minted per installation. Authentication of *callers* is via a static bearer token plus an optional caller-repository allowlist. The Azure Key Vault stores the App's PEM private key; Managed Identity authenticates to Key Vault.

This is a proof-of-concept, so the scope is intentionally small: two read-only MCP tools (`get_file_contents`, `search_code`), a clean GitHub auth pipeline that handles multi-installation correctly, health endpoints, and thorough tests.

## Confirmed design decisions (from the user)

| Decision | Choice |
| --- | --- |
| How the caller identifies its own repo | Custom HTTP header `X-Caller-Repository: owner/repo` |
| GitHub REST API client | **Octokit.NET** — built-in GitHub App + installation token support, mature, covers `get_file_contents` (`RepositoryContentsClient`) and `search_code` (`SearchClient`) |
| Deployment target | Deployment-agnostic — Dockerfile + docs only, no IaC |
| Dockerfile in initial scope | Yes |

## Solution architecture

### Solution layout

```
multirepo-mcp/
├── MultiRepoMcp.sln
├── Dockerfile
├── .dockerignore
├── .editorconfig
├── Directory.Build.props          # shared compiler settings (nullable, warnings-as-errors)
├── Directory.Packages.props       # central package version management
├── README.md                      # already exists; update Setup/Usage sections
├── src/
│   └── MultiRepoMcp/
│       ├── MultiRepoMcp.csproj
│       ├── Program.cs
│       ├── appsettings.json
│       ├── appsettings.Development.json
│       ├── Configuration/
│       │   ├── AuthenticationOptions.cs    # bearer token, allowlist
│       │   ├── GitHubAppOptions.cs         # app id, key vault uri, secret name, refresh threshold
│       │   └── CacheOptions.cs             # installation discovery TTL
│       ├── Authentication/
│       │   ├── StaticBearerAuthenticationHandler.cs
│       │   └── CallerRepositoryAuthorizationHandler.cs   # policy-based allowlist
│       ├── GitHub/
│       │   ├── IGitHubAppJwtFactory.cs / GitHubAppJwtFactory.cs
│       │   ├── IGitHubPrivateKeyProvider.cs / KeyVaultPrivateKeyProvider.cs
│       │   ├── IInstallationResolver.cs / InstallationResolver.cs   # owner/repo → installation_id (cached + negative-cached 60s)
│       │   ├── IInstallationTokenCache.cs / InstallationTokenCache.cs   # (installation_id, repo) → repo-scoped IAT (single-flight, proactive refresh + jitter)
│       │   ├── IGitHubClientFactory.cs / GitHubClientFactory.cs    # per-installation Octokit GitHubClient over shared HttpClientAdapter
│       │   └── Exceptions/AppNotInstalledException.cs
│       ├── Mcp/
│       │   ├── McpToolErrorMapper.cs       # exception → MCP tool error text/status
│       │   └── Tools/
│       │       ├── GetFileContentsTool.cs
│       │       └── SearchCodeTool.cs
│       ├── HealthChecks/
│       │   ├── GitHubHealthCheck.cs        # App-JWT-authenticated GET /app
│       │   └── KeyVaultHealthCheck.cs      # GetPropertiesOfSecret (metadata only)
│       └── Logging/
│           └── SerilogConfiguration.cs
└── tests/
    ├── MultiRepoMcp.UnitTests/
    │   └── MultiRepoMcp.UnitTests.csproj
    └── MultiRepoMcp.IntegrationTests/
        └── MultiRepoMcp.IntegrationTests.csproj   # WebApplicationFactory + WireMock.Net for GitHub
```

### Request lifecycle

```
HTTP request → Serilog request log
            → ASP.NET Core authentication: StaticBearerAuthenticationHandler validates Authorization: Bearer <token>
            → ASP.NET Core authorization: "RequireAllowlistedCaller" policy reads X-Caller-Repository
              and (if allowlist is configured) verifies it's allowed
            → MCP endpoint (mapped via ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore)
            → Tool handler (GetFileContentsTool / SearchCodeTool)
                ↳ InstallationResolver.Resolve(owner, repo) → installation_id
                    ↳ Octokit GitHubAppsClient.GetRepositoryInstallationForCurrent (with App JWT)
                    ↳ 404 → AppNotInstalledException → clear MCP error
                ↳ InstallationTokenCache.GetTokenAsync(installation_id, repo)
                    ↳ Cache hit + > (refresh-threshold + per-key jitter) → return cached repo-scoped IAT
                    ↳ Else single-flight refresh via Octokit.GitHubAppsClient.CreateInstallationToken
                       with body { repositories: [repo] }  ← scopes the IAT to the target repo only
                ↳ GitHubClientFactory.CreateForInstallation(iat) → Octokit GitHubClient over shared HttpClientAdapter
                ↳ Call RepositoryContentsClient / SearchClient → map to MCP result
            → MCP response
```

### Key components

**Bearer authentication.** Implement a minimal `AuthenticationHandler<AuthenticationSchemeOptions>` that reads the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header and compares the presented token to the configured secret using `System.Security.Cryptography.CryptographicOperations.FixedTimeEquals` over UTF-8 byte arrays (after a length check). **Never** use `==` or `string.Equals` — timing attacks. The configured `BearerToken` is validated at startup as `[Required, MinLength(20)]`; **additionally, startup validation rejects obvious placeholder values** (case-insensitive match against `{"...", "changeme", "todo", "replace-me", "your-token-here"}` plus any value containing only repeated chars like `"aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa"`) so a half-finished config can never accidentally accept an attacker-guessable token. Apply the handler to the MCP endpoints with an `[Authorize]`-equivalent. Health endpoints stay anonymous.

**Caller repository allowlist.** A policy/`IAuthorizationHandler` that reads `X-Caller-Repository` from the request, lowercases/normalizes it, and:
- If `AuthenticationOptions.CallerRepositoryAllowlist` is null/empty → allow (allowlist disabled).
- If header missing while allowlist is enabled → fail with 403.
- If value not in allowlist → fail with 403.

**GitHub App JWT factory.** Loads the PEM private key once at startup from Key Vault via `SecretClient` (which authenticates with `DefaultAzureCredential`/Managed Identity). The PEM is parsed once into an `RSA` instance (via `RSA.ImportFromPem`, available in .NET). Every JWT mint is short-lived (≤10 min as required by GitHub) and freshly signed. **Claims:** `iss = AppId` (the App's numeric id), `iat = now - 60s` (GitHub recommends backdating ~60s to tolerate clock skew between the App server and GitHub), `exp = now + 9 min` (under GitHub's 10-min cap, with a small safety margin). **`sub` is intentionally omitted** — GitHub's App JWT spec only requires `iss`, `iat`, `exp`; an extra `sub` would be silently ignored or cause stricter validators to reject.

**Installation resolver.** Wraps `IGitHubClient` (App-JWT authenticated) and calls `GitHubAppsClient.GetRepositoryInstallationForCurrent(owner, repo)`. Result is cached in `IMemoryCache` keyed by lowercase `"owner/repo"`, with a TTL of 1 hour (configurable). 404 from GitHub → throw `AppNotInstalledException` **and cache the negative result for 60 seconds** (configurable; rationale: a misconfigured or loop-driven caller asking for an uninstalled repo would otherwise hammer GitHub's REST API on every call).

**IAT cache (repository-scoped tokens).** The cache key is **`(installationId, repoNameLower)`** — i.e., a `ConcurrentDictionary<(long, string), Lazy<Task<CachedToken>>>`. IATs are minted with the `repositories: [repo]` parameter on `POST /app/installations/{id}/access_tokens` so each token can access **only** the specific target repo. This is the primary defense for the `search_code` "query-escape" risk: even a maliciously-crafted query string cannot reach other repos in the same installation, because the IAT itself is scoped down at GitHub's authoritative boundary. GitHub rejects the mint with 422 if the installation doesn't have access to the named repo, which we map to `AppNotInstalledException` (same surfaced error as the installation-discovery 404).

Single-flight refresh: concurrent gets for the same `(installation, repo)` collapse to a single in-flight refresh. Tokens are returned from cache when the remaining lifetime exceeds the refresh threshold (default 5 minutes, **plus per-entry jitter of ±30s** so many simultaneously-aging entries don't all refresh in the same tick — thundering-herd defense).

**Read/refresh loop (avoiding TOCTOU on the lifetime check).** The naive pattern "read entry → check lifetime → call `TryUpdate(key, new, observed)`" has a race: two callers can both observe the same near-expiry entry, each create a `newLazy`, and call `TryUpdate`. The first one's `TryUpdate` succeeds and replaces the cache entry; the second's `TryUpdate` correctly **fails** because the comparand no longer matches — but that caller still has its own `newLazy` and must not start an independent refresh. The correct pattern is a small loop, **with the refresh task using `CancellationToken.None`** (NEVER the request's cancellation token — a single cancelled caller must not abort a refresh that other callers are awaiting):

```csharp
while (true)
{
    var current = cache.GetOrAdd(key, _ =>
        new Lazy<Task<CachedToken>>(
            () => RefreshAsync(CancellationToken.None),   // shared work is uncancellable per caller
            LazyThreadSafetyMode.ExecutionAndPublication));

    CachedToken token;
    try
    {
        token = await current.Value.ConfigureAwait(false);
    }
    catch
    {
        // Only remove this specific Lazy; do not stomp on a peer's fresh entry.
        cache.TryRemove(new KeyValuePair<(long, string), Lazy<Task<CachedToken>>>(key, current));
        throw;
    }

    if (token.RemainingLifetime > EffectiveRefreshThreshold(key))
        return token;

    var replacement = new Lazy<Task<CachedToken>>(
        () => RefreshAsync(CancellationToken.None),
        LazyThreadSafetyMode.ExecutionAndPublication);
    cache.TryUpdate(key, replacement, current);
    // Either we won (next iteration reads `replacement`) or someone else replaced it (next iteration reads theirs).
}
```

`EffectiveRefreshThreshold(key)` = base threshold (5 min) + a per-key deterministic jitter ∈ [0, 30s] derived from a stable hash of the key. Deterministic jitter (same key → same offset) keeps the cache decision idempotent across concurrent callers but spreads refresh times across the population of installations.

**Failed-refresh handling (avoid `Lazy` poisoning).** A faulted `Task` inside a `Lazy<Task<T>>` is permanent — every subsequent `.Value` returns the same faulted task. The catch block above uses `cache.TryRemove(KeyValuePair<,>)` (the overload that compares **both** key and value) so we only remove the exact poisoned entry; a peer's freshly-installed replacement is left untouched. The next caller's `GetOrAdd` then creates a brand-new `Lazy`. Choosing `LazyThreadSafetyMode.ExecutionAndPublication` (the default) is fine here because the `TryRemove` is the recovery path — we don't rely on `Lazy`'s `PublicationOnly` re-execution semantics.

**`CachedToken` semantics (pinned to remove parsing ambiguity).** `CachedToken` carries `string Token` and `DateTimeOffset ExpiresAtUtc` (parsed from GitHub's `expires_at` ISO-8601 string). `RemainingLifetime => max(TimeSpan.Zero, ExpiresAtUtc - DateTimeOffset.UtcNow)`. Time source is `DateTimeOffset.UtcNow` directly in production, but the cache takes an `Func<DateTimeOffset>` (or `TimeProvider`) injected via DI so tests can advance time deterministically.

**Stale installation-ID detection.** When `InstallationResolver` performs a fresh discovery (cache miss or TTL expiry) and the returned installation ID differs from a previously cached value for the same `owner/repo`, the resolver calls `IInstallationTokenCache.Invalidate(oldInstallationId, repo)` before returning. Covers the "app uninstalled then reinstalled, new installation ID" case.

No background timer — refresh is lazy/on-demand.

**GitHub HTTP transport (shared `HttpClient`).** Octokit's default `GitHubClient(ProductHeaderValue)` constructor creates a fresh `HttpClient` + handler per instance. Constructing a new `GitHubClient` per request would churn sockets under load and lose connection pooling. Solution: register a singleton `HttpMessageHandler` (or use `IHttpClientFactory` with a named client) and pass it into Octokit via `new HttpClientAdapter(() => sharedHandler)`. The `Connection` is then either a singleton (its `Credentials` get overwritten per-request) or constructed per request over the shared adapter. We use the second pattern: a singleton `HttpClientAdapter`, fresh `Connection` per request configured with the IAT-bearing `Credentials`, and a fresh `GitHubClient` wrapping that `Connection`. Only the lightweight wrapper objects are allocated per request; the underlying handler (with its connection pool) is shared.

**GitHub client factory.** Two flavors, both built on the shared `HttpClientAdapter`:
1. **App-JWT client** — rebuilt with a fresh JWT per call (JWTs expire in 10 min). Used by `InstallationResolver` and the GitHub health check.
2. **Installation client** — constructed per request: `new GitHubClient(new Connection(ProductHeaderValue("multirepo-mcp"), GitHubClient.GitHubApiUrl, new InMemoryCredentialStore(new Credentials(iat.Token)), sharedAdapter, new SimpleJsonSerializer()))`. The lifetime cost is one allocation; HTTP connections come from the singleton pool.

**MCP host setup (verified against the official C# SDK).** Use the `ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore` package (currently v1.3.0+, supports `net10.0`). The host is wired as:

```csharp
builder.Services.AddMcpServer()
    .WithHttpTransport(options =>
    {
        // Stateless mode supports horizontal scaling and avoids per-session
        // in-memory state. Our tools are request/response, so we don't need
        // sampling, elicitation, or other server-to-client requests.
        options.Stateless = true;
    })
    .WithTools<GetFileContentsTool>()
    .WithTools<SearchCodeTool>();

// ... auth registration (custom static bearer scheme) ...
app.UseAuthentication();
app.UseAuthorization();
app.MapMcp("/mcp").RequireAuthorization("RequireAllowlistedCaller");
```

Tools are classes decorated with `[McpServerToolType]`; tool methods are decorated with `[McpServerTool]` + `[Description("...")]`. Parameter docs come from `[Description]` on each parameter — this maps directly to the MCP tool input schema.

The streamable HTTP endpoint is mapped at **`/mcp`** (not the SDK default `/`) so the root doesn't collide with health endpoints or any future static routes and so reverse-proxy path-prefix rewrites have an unambiguous segment to target. Health endpoints stay anonymous at `/health/*`. An integration test asserts that an MCP `tools/list` round-trip returns **both** `get_file_contents` and `search_code` — this catches `.WithTools<T>()` chaining regressions and accidental discovery failures.

**MCP tool methods.** Methods on `GetFileContentsTool` / `SearchCodeTool` accept `owner` and `repo` parameters. Strongly-typed responses; map Octokit exceptions to typed MCP errors in a small helper.

- `get_file_contents(owner, repo, path, ref?)` → calls `RepositoryContentsClient.GetAllContentsByRef` if `ref` provided, else `GetAllContents`. Returns content as text when MIME/encoding indicates text; otherwise return base64 + size + sha (and a note that the content is binary).
  - **Size cap (size-first, decode-second):** the Contents API returns `size` in the response envelope before the (base64) content. Check `size` against the 1 MiB cap *before* attempting to decode the content payload; for entries above the cap return a typed "file too large" error without decoding. This avoids wasted CPU and memory on large blobs.
  - **Path validation (defense in depth):** before any GitHub call, reject `path` if it is null/empty, contains any `\0` or `char.IsControl(c)`, contains a `\\`, starts with `/`, or exceeds 1024 characters.
  - **`ref` validation:** if provided, reject if null/empty, contains any control char, contains a space, or exceeds 250 characters (well above any real GitHub ref length).
  - **Owner/repo validation:** match against `^[A-Za-z0-9](?:[A-Za-z0-9-]{0,38})?$` for owner and the GitHub repo-name regex for repo; reject before any cache lookup.
  - **Non-file entries (explicit handling):** the Contents API can return a directory listing (array), a submodule, or a symlink. We treat these explicitly rather than silently returning the wrong thing:
    - **Directory** (response is a JSON array) → return a typed `"PathIsDirectory"` MCP error with a short message ("path resolves to a directory; directory listing is not supported in this version"). Directory listing is a deliberate non-goal for the POC.
    - **Submodule** (`type: "submodule"`) → return a typed `"PathIsSubmodule"` error including the submodule's `submodule_git_url` and `sha` so the caller can fetch from the submodule's source repo separately.
    - **Symlink** (`type: "symlink"`) → return the symlink's `target` (a repo-relative path string) in a typed `"PathIsSymlink"` response. We intentionally do NOT auto-follow the symlink in the POC — auto-follow would need cycle detection and depth bounds. The caller can re-invoke `get_file_contents` with the resolved target if they want.

- `search_code(owner, repo, query, max_results?)` → calls `SearchClient.SearchCode` with `new SearchCodeRequest(query) { Repos = new RepositoryCollection { { owner, repo } } }`. Cap results (default 30, max 100). Return path, repo, sha, html_url, and a short text fragment per match. **The IAT used is repo-scoped (see "IAT cache" above), so even a maliciously-crafted query can never reach repos outside the target — the security boundary is enforced by GitHub at the token level, not by our query sanitization.** Query sanitization below is defense-in-depth.
  - **Indexing constraint (documented):** GitHub's code-search API only returns results from *indexed* repositories. For org-owned repos this is generally automatic; for personal/user repos the owner must opt in. When the API returns an empty result for a repo that *should* match, the response should hint at this (e.g., "no results; if the repo is unindexed, code search will return nothing"). The REST code-search API also only indexes the default branch and excludes files above GitHub's size threshold (currently 384 KB) — documented in the tool description so callers understand the limits.
  - **Incomplete-results signal:** the Octokit `SearchCodeResult.IncompleteResults` boolean (mapping GitHub's `incomplete_results` field) is propagated to the MCP response so callers can tell the difference between "no matches" and "search timed out before completing".
  - **Query-scope hardening (defense in depth):** the caller's `query` string is rejected if it contains, anywhere outside a double-quoted literal:
    - **Any GitHub search qualifier** matched by case-insensitive regex `\b[a-z][a-z0-9_-]*:` (covers `repo:`, `user:`, `org:`, `owner:`, `is:`, `in:`, `path:`, `language:`, `extension:`, `filename:`, `size:`, plus any future qualifier — much more robust than a hand-picked allowlist).
    - **Boolean operators / negation:** standalone `OR` or `NOT` tokens (case-sensitive — GitHub's DSL is case-sensitive on these), or a leading `-` on any whitespace-delimited token (the GitHub "exclude this term" syntax).
    Rejected queries map to an `ArgumentException` with a clear MCP error: "search_code queries cannot include scope qualifiers, boolean operators (OR/NOT), or leading-dash exclusions. The repository scope is set by the tool's `owner`/`repo` parameters; use double quotes for literal terms containing `:` or `-`."

**Error handling.**
- 401 on missing/invalid bearer.
- 403 when caller repo allowlist denies.
- MCP tool errors (returned in-band as MCP tool results, not HTTP errors) for:
  - App-not-installed on target repo (exact wording from the PRD). Triggered by either: (a) `InstallationResolver` 404 from `GetRepositoryInstallationForCurrent`, or (b) IAT mint 422 with `repositories` body indicating the named repo isn't accessible to the installation.
  - GitHub API 404 on file/path → "File not found".
  - GitHub Search API **422** (`ApiException` with status `UnprocessableEntity`) → "Invalid search query" with the GitHub-provided error message surfaced verbatim (helpful for callers debugging quoting issues).
  - GitHub API **403 secondary rate limit / abuse-detection** (`SecondaryRateLimitExceededException` if available in the Octokit version; otherwise an `ApiException` with `RateLimit` info absent and `Retry-After` header present) → distinct "Secondary rate limit" message with retry-after, kept separate from auth-403.
  - GitHub API primary rate limit (`RateLimitExceededException`) → clear retry-after message with the reset timestamp.
  - GitHub API auth failure (`AuthorizationException`) → log + return generic "GitHub authentication failed".
  - `get_file_contents` non-file entries → `PathIsDirectory` / `PathIsSubmodule` / `PathIsSymlink` (typed payloads, see Tool methods section).
  - Validation failures (bad `owner/repo` format, malformed `path`/`ref`, empty `query`, query qualifier injection) → MCP error before any GitHub call.
- Unhandled exceptions → 500 with correlation id; never leak stack traces or tokens.

**Transport-level security hardening (from the official SDK security guidance).**
- **`AllowedHosts`** — configure ASP.NET Core's host filtering to a known list rather than `"*"`. Defends against DNS rebinding when the server is reachable on a loopback or non-public address. Set `AllowedHosts` in `appsettings.json` (root-level key consumed by `WebApplicationBuilder` defaults). ASP.NET Core's built-in `HostFilteringMiddleware` is auto-registered by `Host.CreateDefaultBuilder` / `WebApplication.CreateBuilder` and runs before authentication, so no manual `UseHostFiltering()` call is required — but we will add an integration test that asserts a request with an unknown `Host` header is rejected before reaching auth, to make sure no one disables this in the future. Defaults: production hostname in prod, `"localhost;127.0.0.1;[::1]"` in dev.
- **CORS** — **intentionally disabled**. MCP clients (Copilot agents, custom CLIs) are not browsers; enabling CORS only widens the attack surface. If a browser-based use case appears later, add a restrictive `WithOrigins(...)` policy and wire it via `MapMcp().RequireCors("...")` per the SDK sample.
- **HTTPS** — server expects to run behind a TLS-terminating proxy (Container Apps / App Service / ingress); document this in the README. `UseHttpsRedirection()` enabled when not in dev.

**Health checks.** Three endpoints:
- `/health/live` — liveness, just `200 OK`. **Dependency-free** (never touches Key Vault or GitHub). Important: orchestrators (Kubernetes, Container Apps) treat liveness failures as "restart the container"; coupling liveness to external dependencies would cause restart storms during transient GitHub/KV blips.
- `/health/ready` — readiness: checks Key Vault (`GetPropertiesOfSecretAsync` on the private-key secret) and GitHub (`GET /app` with the App JWT). Both must pass.
- `/health/startup` — same as ready, runs once during container startup.

**Health-check result caching (rate-limit defense).** Orchestrators typically probe readiness every 5–10 seconds. Uncached, this would burn GitHub primary rate-limit budget (5000/hr/installation, but App JWT calls are billed per App against a separate budget) and Key Vault transactional cost on every poll, and would make the server's health flap with any transient GitHub blip. **The readiness check therefore caches its dependency-check results for 30 seconds** (configurable) using the .NET `HealthCheckOptions.Period` mechanism (for `AddCheck`) combined with internal per-check result caching. Each dependency check additionally has a **3-second timeout**; if a check times out we mark it unhealthy for the cache TTL rather than holding the probe open. This means the *worst-case* GitHub `GET /app` load from health checks is ~2 calls/min regardless of probe frequency.

**Health check response sanitization.** Responses use the default `HealthCheckOptions.ResponseWriter` configured to emit `{"status":"Healthy|Unhealthy"}` only — no individual check names, no `Description`/`Exception`/`Data` fields, no App ID, no Key Vault URI. Detailed diagnostics (which dependency failed, why, correlation id) are written to the server log instead.

**Logging.** Serilog with console sink (JSON in prod, plain text in dev). Enrich every request scope with: correlation id, caller-repository, target `owner/repo` (when known), installation id (when known). **Never log the bearer token or the IAT itself.**

**Correlation IDs.** A small request middleware reads `X-Correlation-Id` from the request if present (sanitised to `[A-Za-z0-9._-]{1,128}`); otherwise generates a fresh `Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N")`. The value is echoed back via the `X-Correlation-Id` response header and used as the Serilog scope key.

**Configuration.**
```jsonc
{
  "AllowedHosts": "multirepo-mcp.example.com",   // host filtering (DNS-rebinding defense)
  "Authentication": {
    "BearerToken": "...",                       // from env var / Key Vault in prod; [Required, MinLength(20)]
    "CallerRepositoryAllowlist": ["owner/repo"] // optional; empty/null disables the check
  },
  "GitHubApp": {
    "AppId": 123456,
    "KeyVaultUri": "https://...vault.azure.net/",
    "PrivateKeySecretName": "multirepo-mcp-private-key",
    "InstallationTokenRefreshThreshold": "00:05:00"
  },
  "Cache": {
    "InstallationDiscoveryTtl": "01:00:00"
  },
  "Serilog": { /* standard Serilog config */ }
}
```
Bind via `IOptions<T>`; validate on startup with data annotations + `ValidateOnStart()`. Layer order: `appsettings.json` → `appsettings.{Environment}.json` → env vars → (optionally) Azure Key Vault.

### Testing strategy

**Critical concurrency tests for IAT cache (highest design risk):**
- 10 concurrent gets on a cold cache for the same `(installation, repo)` → exactly 1 underlying refresh.
- 10 concurrent gets for *different* `(installation, repo)` pairs → each gets its own independent refresh; no head-of-line blocking.
- Refresh throws (e.g., GitHub 503) → cache entry is removed; the next call performs a fresh refresh and succeeds.
- Refresh throws on caller A while caller B has *already* installed a successful replacement → A's `TryRemove(KeyValuePair<,>)` is a no-op and B's fresh entry stays in the cache (verifies the key+value `TryRemove` overload, not the bare `TryRemove(key)`).
- **Caller cancels mid-refresh:** caller A awaits the shared refresh with a `CancellationToken` that fires, while caller B awaits the same refresh without cancellation → A observes `OperationCanceledException` from *its own await*, but the shared refresh task continues to completion and B receives the token (proves we did not pipe A's CT into `RefreshAsync`).
- Refresh returns a token within the refresh threshold of expiry → the very next call immediately refreshes again (proactive refresh).
- Refresh returns a token well above the threshold → subsequent calls within the window all hit the cache (zero refreshes).
- **Jitter spread:** seed two `(installation, repo)` pairs with tokens that expire at the same wall-clock time and assert their effective refresh thresholds differ (i.e., they don't both flip stale on exactly the same `now`). Verifies jitter is per-key deterministic and non-zero.
- **Bounded stress** of 1,000 iterations per assertion (parameterised; **not** 10k — 10k is slow/flaky and the determinism we care about is already covered by the targeted interleaving tests above). Tests run with `TimeProvider`-mocked clocks to remove real delays.
- `TimeProvider` injection lets the tests advance time across the proactive-refresh boundary deterministically.
- `InstallationResolver` returns a different installation id after TTL expiry → `IInstallationTokenCache.Invalidate(oldId, repo)` is called exactly once.

**Unit tests (xUnit + Moq + FluentAssertions):**
- `StaticBearerAuthenticationHandler`: valid token / wrong token / missing header / empty configured token (should reject); **placeholder-value rejection at startup** (`"changeme"`, `"..."`, all-same-char) → `OptionsValidationException`.
- Caller allowlist authorization handler: allowlist disabled / header missing / not in allowlist / in allowlist / case-insensitivity.
- `GitHubAppJwtFactory`: signs with the right `alg`/`iss`/`iat`/`exp`; **`sub` is absent**; `iat` is ~60s in the past; respects clock skew; rebuilds JWT each call (doesn't reuse expired).
- `KeyVaultPrivateKeyProvider`: pulls the secret via a mocked `SecretClient`; surfaces friendly errors on Key Vault failure.
- `InstallationResolver`: cache hit / cache miss / 404 → `AppNotInstalledException` + negative-cached for 60s / concurrent calls coalesce / stale-id detection invalidates IAT cache.
- `InstallationTokenCache`: returns cached token when fresh; refreshes when within threshold; **single-flight** test (10 concurrent gets → 1 refresh call); independent `(installation, repo)` pairs refresh independently; IAT mint 422 ("repository not accessible") → `AppNotInstalledException`.
- `GitHubClientFactory`: a single shared `HttpClientAdapter` is reused across N installation-client creations (verify by inspecting the underlying handler reference).
- `GetFileContentsTool`: success (text), success (binary → base64), 404 → "not found", malformed `owner/repo`, path validation (null, empty, leading `/`, `\\`, control chars, > 1024 chars), `ref` validation (null=ok if omitted, empty=reject, contains space=reject, control chars=reject, > 250 chars=reject). Size cap boundaries: exactly `MaxSize - 1`, exactly `MaxSize`, and `MaxSize + 1` (first two succeed, third returns the typed "file too large" error **and asserts the response payload was never base64-decoded** — verified by checking the path through a fake `RepositoryContentsClient` that records whether `.Content` was accessed). **Non-file entries:** directory response → `PathIsDirectory` MCP error; submodule → `PathIsSubmodule` with `submodule_git_url` + `sha`; symlink → `PathIsSymlink` with `target`.
- `SearchCodeTool`: success, empty query, result capping, scoping (query passed only the target repo's qualifier), **query rejection** for case-insensitive variants of every qualifier (`repo:`, `path:`, `language:`, `extension:`, `org:`, ...), boolean operators (`OR`, `NOT`), leading-dash exclusions (`-keyword`), and combinations with surrounding whitespace; **double-quoted literal exception** (e.g., `"foo:bar"` as a search term must NOT be rejected). Verify `IncompleteResults` is propagated.
- `McpToolErrorMapper`: each exception type → expected MCP error shape; 422 → "Invalid search query"; secondary rate limit → distinct from primary rate limit and from auth failure.

**Integration tests (WebApplicationFactory + WireMock.Net):**
- Spin up the full server with `WebApplicationFactory<Program>`, replacing the GitHub HTTP client base address with a WireMock.Net server that stubs the relevant GitHub REST endpoints. Replace `KeyVaultPrivateKeyProvider` with a test provider that returns a generated RSA key.
- **MCP `tools/list` round-trip** → response includes BOTH `get_file_contents` and `search_code` with non-empty descriptions and parameter schemas (catches `.WithTools<T>()` chaining/discovery regressions).
- End-to-end MCP flow: send an MCP request to `get_file_contents`, assert WireMock saw a `GET /repos/{o}/{r}/installation` then a `POST /app/installations/{id}/access_tokens` **with body `{"repositories": ["{r}"]}`** then a `GET /repos/{o}/{r}/contents/{path}` with the IAT.
- **Repo-scoping enforcement:** in the body of the access-token POST WireMock verifies `repositories` contains the target repo and only the target repo.
- Bearer rejected → 401.
- Allowlist deny → 403.
- Allowlist allow → 200.
- **Host filtering pre-auth:** request with an unknown `Host` header → rejected before the bearer is even checked (assert no bearer-validation log entry).
- Cross-repo within same installation: two different `owner/repo`s under the same installation each get their own repo-scoped IAT (different cache entries).
- Cross-installation: two different `owner/repo`s map to two different installation IDs, each gets its own IAT, IAT cache returns the right token per request.
- App-not-installed: WireMock returns 404 on `/repos/.../installation` → MCP tool error has the PRD's exact wording. Also: WireMock returns 422 on the access-token POST → same error wording (the IAT-mint path of the app-not-installed signal).
- **Health-check caching:** with WireMock recording every GitHub call, fire 60 readiness probes in 30 seconds and assert WireMock saw exactly **one** `GET /app` (proves the 30s result cache works).
- Health endpoints: `/health/live` always 200 (and never calls WireMock); `/health/ready` reflects mocked Key Vault / GitHub health.

### Documentation (README updates)

Fill in the `## Setup` and `## Usage` sections currently marked `// TODO`. Specifically:
- GitHub App registration steps (permissions: `contents: read`; no webhook; no callback).
- Storing the PEM in Key Vault and granting the app's Managed Identity `Get` on secrets.
- Configuration reference table for every key in `appsettings.json` and its env-var equivalent.
- Bearer token + allowlist explanation, with an example `X-Caller-Repository` header.
- How to run locally (with a placeholder bearer + a dev PEM in user-secrets / env var bypassing Key Vault).
- How to build & run the Docker image.
- MCP tool reference (`get_file_contents`, `search_code`) with example MCP requests.
- Health endpoint reference.

### Dockerfile

Multi-stage build:
1. `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:10.0` for restore/publish (AOT or framework-dependent — start with framework-dependent for simplicity).
2. `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:10.0` as runtime, non-root user, expose 8080, `ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "MultiRepoMcp.dll"]`.

`.dockerignore` to exclude `bin/`, `obj/`, `tests/`, `.git/`, `prompts/`, etc.

## Notes, risks, and open questions

- **Known authorization limitation (acknowledged, by design for POC).** The bearer token is shared and the `X-Caller-Repository` header is self-attested, so any holder of a valid bearer can request any repo where the App is installed. The repo-scoped IAT design above ensures that scope can't be widened *within* a single tool call (the IAT only sees the target repo), but it does NOT prevent one caller from cycling through other allowlisted repos. **For the POC this is explicitly acceptable** (PRD only requires bearer + optional inbound allowlist). If per-caller target restrictions become needed, the natural extension is either (a) a `BearerToken → AllowedTargetRepos` map in configuration and a check at the start of every tool method, or (b) per-caller bearer tokens issued via a gateway. The current code structure already passes `(targetOwner, targetRepo)` through the authorization layer, so adding (a) is a single new policy handler — no rewiring of the auth pipeline. The README will document this explicitly under a "Threat model" subsection.
- **`ModelContextProtocol` SDK surface (verified May 2026).** Confirmed against the official C# SDK docs (https://csharp.sdk.modelcontextprotocol.io/) and the `ProtectedMcpServer` sample: `AddMcpServer().WithHttpTransport(o => o.Stateless = true).WithTools<T>()`, `[McpServerToolType]` + `[McpServerTool]`, `app.MapMcp().RequireAuthorization(...)`. Package versions ≥ 1.3.0 target `net10.0`. The MCP SDK's `.AddMcp(...)` auth extension is OAuth-flavored (publishes `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`); since we use a static bearer token (not OAuth), we'll register a *custom* `AuthenticationHandler` instead of `.AddMcp()` to keep the surface honest. The `tools/list` integration test guards against silent regressions in tool discovery from chained `.WithTools<T>()` calls.
- **.NET 10 baseline image availability.** Plan assumes `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/{sdk,aspnet}:10.0` images are available; we'll verify during Dockerfile work.
- **PEM key rotation.** Out of scope for the POC — key is loaded once at startup. Documented in README as a known limitation; restart picks up rotation.
- **Caller-repository normalization.** Lowercased before allowlist comparison; GitHub itself is case-insensitive on owner/repo. The `X-Caller-Repository` value is **not** trusted to authorize against the *target* repo — it only gates inbound access (see "Known authorization limitation" above).
- **GitHub code-search REST API limitations.** Default branch only; files above ~384 KB are not indexed; small primary rate-limit budget (30/min unauthenticated, 30/min per App for search). Documented in the `search_code` tool description so callers don't expect filesystem-wide grep behavior.
- **No webhook / no incoming installation events.** The installation discovery cache TTL is the only invalidation mechanism for uninstall/reinstall; that's acceptable for a POC.
- **Octokit version pinning.** `SecondaryRateLimitExceededException` was added in Octokit 9.0+. We'll pin Octokit ≥ 9.0 in `Directory.Packages.props`; if for some reason we have to support an older version, the error mapper falls back to inspecting `ApiException.StatusCode == Forbidden` + presence of a `Retry-After` header.

## Todos (tracked in SQL)

1. **scaffold** — Solution + projects + central package mgmt + editorconfig + Directory.Build.props
2. **config-options** — `AuthenticationOptions`, `GitHubAppOptions`, `CacheOptions` + binding + validation
3. **bearer-auth** — `StaticBearerAuthenticationHandler` + auth scheme registration
4. **caller-allowlist** — Authorization handler/policy reading `X-Caller-Repository`
5. **keyvault-key** — `KeyVaultPrivateKeyProvider` using Managed Identity / `DefaultAzureCredential`
6. **jwt-factory** — `GitHubAppJwtFactory` producing 10-min RS256 JWTs
7. **installation-resolver** — `InstallationResolver` with `IMemoryCache` + `AppNotInstalledException`
8. **iat-cache** — `InstallationTokenCache` with single-flight + proactive refresh
9. **client-factory** — `GitHubClientFactory` (App-JWT client + per-installation client)
10. **mcp-host** — Register `ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore` MCP host + map endpoint behind auth
11. **tool-get-file** — `GetFileContentsTool` with size caps + binary handling
12. **tool-search-code** — `SearchCodeTool` with result caps + repo-scoping
13. **error-mapper** — `McpToolErrorMapper` (Octokit exceptions → MCP errors)
14. **logging** — Serilog setup, request enrichment, secret-redaction safety
15. **health-checks** — `/health/live`, `/health/ready`, `/health/startup` + `GitHubHealthCheck` + `KeyVaultHealthCheck`
16. **unit-tests** — All unit test classes listed above
17. **integration-tests** — WebApplicationFactory + WireMock.Net end-to-end tests
18. **dockerfile** — Multi-stage Dockerfile + `.dockerignore`
19. **readme** — Fill in Setup, Usage, configuration reference, tool reference, health reference, **Multi-Installation Handling** subsection (mirroring the PRD: per-installation IAT cache, single-flight refresh, proactive refresh, app-not-installed error wording, installation discovery cache TTL and tuning)

Dependencies (rough):
- All implementation tasks depend on `scaffold`.
- `jwt-factory` depends on `keyvault-key`.
- `installation-resolver` depends on `jwt-factory` + `client-factory` (App-JWT flavor).
- `iat-cache` depends on `client-factory` (App-JWT flavor).
- `client-factory` (installation flavor) depends on `iat-cache`.
- Tools (`tool-get-file`, `tool-search-code`) depend on `installation-resolver` + `iat-cache` + `client-factory`.
- `mcp-host` depends on `bearer-auth` + `caller-allowlist`.
- `error-mapper` is needed before tools land their final error paths.
- `health-checks` depends on `keyvault-key` + `client-factory` (App-JWT flavor).
- `unit-tests` and `integration-tests` can be developed alongside the code they cover; integration tests depend on the full host being wired up.
- `dockerfile` depends on `scaffold` (needs the publishable project).
- `readme` depends on basically everything else so the docs reflect the implementation.
